free_text: Spin cutoff factor and level density for 59Ni from an
analysis of compound nuclear reactions
The swinger facility of the tandem
accelerator at the Edwards Accelerator Laboratory,
Ohio University.
Approved by A.V.Voinov on January 23, 2025.
BP
The liquid scintillator NE213 detector of 5x2
in. was used for neutron detection.
About 0.5 mg/cm2 thick enriched
to 95% (56Fe) was used.
Neutron energies were determined by time of
flight technique with a pulsed beam of about 3 ns
timing width. For the 56Fe target, neutron spectra
were measured at 20, 34, 45, 62, 76, 118, 135, and
146 degree angles in the laboratory frame.
Spectra were corrected on detector efficiency, and
converted to the center of mass frame.
Because it is difficult to estimate systematic
uncertainties, the following procedure to estimate
combined statistical and systematical uncertainties
was adopted: the experimental angular distribution
was fitted with a second order polynomial P(cos
theta) function and the value of dW exp was
calculated as dW exp = (1/N) *
*sqrt(Sigma N ((Pexp(cos theta) - P(cos theta))**2)).
Combined statistical and systematical
uncertainties.
Author's
data. Also shown in Fig. 1a.
Angular distributions were extracted for the neutron
energy intervals (E1,E2 ): (2.5,3.4), (3.4,4.4), and
(4.4,7.8) MeV. The first two intervals correspond to
the population of the continuum region at excitation
energies higher than energies of known discrete levels
of 59Ni. The last interval corresponds to population of
discrete levels which are known in the excitation
energy range up to about 3.3 MeV.
No information on source of uncertainties.
Author's
data. Also shown in Fig. 1b.

Author's
data. Also shown in Fig. 1b.
Experimental
details.
The targets foils were enriched iron isotopes of
about 0.5 mg/cm2 thickness placed in the center of
the target chamber.
The proton spectra were measured at backward
157 and 146 degrees angles. The low energy protons (
from 2 to 10 MeV) were selected by registering time
of flight and the energy deposited in Si detectors
mounted at the end of the 2m and 1m flight path
tubes. The high energy protons (from 7 to 30 MeV)
were measured by dE-E Si telescope placed at the 150
degree angle in respect to the beam line. The
telescope was placed at the distance of about 15cm
from the target to increase the solid angle. The
proton spectrum was constructed from low and high
energy parts matching in the region of their
overlapping.
No information on source of uncertainties.
Author's
data. Also shown in Fig. 2a.
